mainText: Good morning. Here is your curated AI briefing for Wednesday, July 22, 2026. Google DeepMind is expanding its contribution to the United States government’s Genesis Mission with a $40 million commitment in cloud credits and AI tokens. This initiative aims to accelerate scientific discovery across the 17 Department of Energy National Laboratories. By providing researchers with access to specialized tools, including the protein-structure predictor AlphaFold 3 and the AI coding agent AlphaEvolve, Google intends to support complex research in areas like fusion plasma dynamics and materials science. This matters because it integrates frontier AI directly into the federal laboratory infrastructure, potentially shortening discovery timelines that previously took years. Initial applications are already showing results; for example, researchers at the National Laboratory of the Rockies reported that using Gemini to automate microscope calibration reduced setup time from 90 minutes to 13 minutes. In infrastructure developments, Wistron has opened a 324,000-square-foot manufacturing plant in Fort Worth, Texas, to produce advanced NVIDIA AI systems, including the GB300 Grace Blackwell Ultra Superchip. This $700 million project is part of a broader commitment to establish a domestic manufacturing base for AI hardware, with plans to employ up to 1,000 people by the end of the year. The facility represents an effort to scale production for what NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang described as fundamental infrastructure for the AI era. Separately, OpenAI announced a long-term data center project in Effingham County, Georgia, dubbed Project Camellia. The company committed to paying the full cost of infrastructure and electric service, stating that local electricity rates for residents will not increase. OpenAI also pledged $80 million in community benefits and $71 million in educational credits for students to use its agentic coding tool, Codex. OpenAI also introduced a new enterprise product called Presence. Designed to help businesses deploy and maintain AI agents, the platform includes guardrails, escalation protocols, and an improvement process powered by Codex. The system allows companies to define policies and automate tasks while maintaining human oversight. OpenAI reports that the platform already powers its own internal customer support, resolving 75 percent of inbound issues without human assistance. In the creator economy, the Berlin-based startup Passionfroot raised $15 million in a Series A funding round led by Insight Partners. The company operates a marketplace that connects B2B creators with brands, and it recently launched an AI agent called Zest to help manage campaign performance and discover relevant creators. Passionfroot intends to use these funds to expand its operations into the United States and Brazil. Independent reporting from earlier this week highlights a shift in browser technology, noting that companies are increasingly competing to integrate AI assistants directly into the browsing experience. While Google Chrome and Apple Safari maintain market dominance, new entrants are positioning their browsers as proactive tools. Examples include The Browser Company’s Arc browser, which features an AI-centric interface called Dia, and Opera’s Neon browser, which offers contextual awareness for research and coding tasks. Meanwhile, OpenAI has discontinued its Atlas browser, opting instead to incorporate those agentic capabilities directly into its existing desktop application and Chrome extension. Finally, researchers are increasingly relying on simulation to overcome data scarcity in physical AI. Hugging Face noted in an overview earlier this week that because training physical robots is expensive and risky, developers are turning to GPU-accelerated simulation engines to generate photorealistic, physically grounded data. NVIDIA also released an open-source medical physics simulation framework for its Isaac for Healthcare platform, designed to help developers model anatomy and device interactions in virtual environments before moving to physical testing. This framework allows for the parallel execution of hundreds of simulation environments, which can significantly reduce the time required to train robotic systems. Looking ahead, watch for the public open house in Effingham County, Georgia, scheduled for tomorrow, July 23, where OpenAI will gather community feedback to shape its development commitments. Additionally, monitor how the expansion of agentic browser features influences market share as companies move away from traditional search-centric models toward AI-native automation. That is your curated AI briefing for today.
